stem: Water flows into a tank at a rate of \( r(t) = [[m]]t + [[k]] \) liters per minute, where \( t \) is measured in minutes. Find the net change in the volume of water in the tank from \( t = [[a]] \) to \( t = [[b]] \).
solution: The net change equals the integral of the rate: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}([[m]]t+[[k]])\,dt = \dfrac{[[m]]}{2}\left([[b]]^{2}-[[a]]^{2}\right) + [[k]]([[b]]-[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rate \( r([[b]]) \) at the final time, not the accumulated change.
This uses the final rate times the elapsed time (a right-endpoint rectangle).
This uses the initial rate times the elapsed time (a left-endpoint rectangle).
This subtracts the constant term instead of adding it.
This is off by \( [[m]] \) from the correct net change.

stem: A chemical reaction produces a substance at a rate of \( r(t) = [[p]]t^{2} + ([[q]])t + ([[k]]) \) grams per minute, where \( t \) is time in minutes. Find the net change in the amount of substance produced from \( t = [[a]] \) to \( t = [[b]] \).
solution: The net change equals the integral of the rate: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left([[p]]t^{2}+([[q]])t+([[k]])\right)dt = \dfrac{[[p]]}{3}\left([[b]]^{3}-[[a]]^{3}\right) + \dfrac{[[q]]}{2}\left([[b]]^{2}-[[a]]^{2}\right) + [[k]]([[b]]-[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the rate \( r([[b]]) \) at the final time, not the accumulated change.
This uses the final rate times the elapsed time (a right-endpoint rectangle).
This uses the power rule with denominator 2 instead of 3 for the \( t^{2} \) term.
This mistakenly substitutes \( [[b]]-[[a]] \) for \( t \) instead of evaluating at each endpoint.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct net change.

stem: A tank contains \( [[N0]] \) liters of water at time \( t = 0 \). Water is then added or removed at a rate of \( r(t) = [[m]]t + [[k]] \) liters per minute, for \( t \) in minutes. How many liters of water are in the tank at time \( t = [[b]] \)?
solution: By the net change theorem, the amount at time \( t=[[b]] \) is the initial amount plus the accumulated change: \( [[N0]] + \int_{0}^{[[b]]}([[m]]t+[[k]])\,dt = [[N0]] + \dfrac{[[m]]}{2}[[b]]^{2} + [[k]][[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives only the net change, forgetting to add the initial \( [[N0]] \) liters.
This uses \( r([[b]]) \) instead of integrating the rate to find the accumulated change.
This incorrectly scales the initial amount by the rate instead of adding the accumulated change.
This subtracts the accumulated change instead of adding it.
This is off by \( [[N0]] \) from the correct amount.

stem: Water flows into a reservoir at a rate of \( R_{in}(t) = [[p]]t + [[q]] \) liters per minute and flows out at a rate of \( R_{out}(t) = [[r]]t + [[s]] \) liters per minute, where \( t \) is in minutes. Find the net change in the volume of water in the reservoir from \( t = [[a]] \) to \( t = [[b]] \).
solution: The net rate of change of volume is \( R_{in}(t)-R_{out}(t) \), so the net change is \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left(([[p]]t+[[q]])-([[r]]t+[[s]])\right)dt = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two rates' accumulated amounts instead of subtracting.
This computes outflow minus inflow, the negative of the correct net change.
This evaluates the net rate only at \( t=[[b]] \) instead of integrating it.
This adds the rates instead of subtracting before integrating.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct net change.

stem: The marginal cost of producing \( x \) units of a product is \( C'(x) = \dfrac{[[k]]}{(x+[[c]])^{2}} \) dollars per unit. Find the increase in total cost as production increases from \( x = [[a]] \) to \( x = [[b]] \) units.
solution: An antiderivative of \( \dfrac{[[k]]}{(x+[[c]])^{2}} \) is \( -\dfrac{[[k]]}{x+[[c]]} \), so the increase in cost is \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\dfrac{[[k]]}{(x+[[c]])^{2}}\,dx = \dfrac{[[k]]}{[[a]]+[[c]]} - \dfrac{[[k]]}{[[b]]+[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the endpoints in the wrong order, giving the negative of the correct increase.
This treats the integrand as \( \dfrac{k}{x+c} \) and uses a logarithmic antiderivative instead of the power rule.
This evaluates the marginal cost \( C'(x) \) at both endpoints instead of integrating it.
This uses only the left endpoint's marginal cost times the width, a left-rectangle approximation.
This is off by \( [[k]] \) from the correct increase.
